title: What is a test in Go
instruction:
In Go, a test is just a function with a specific signature:

```
func TestSomething(t *testing.T) {
    // test code here
}
```

Rules:
- File name must end in `_test.go`
- Function name must start with `Test`
- Takes one parameter: `t *testing.T`
- Run all tests with: `go test ./...`

Run this. It has one function being tested (`add`) and one test for it. Notice how `t.Errorf` reports a failure with a formatted message.
successMessage: The test passed — add(2, 3) returned 5 as expected. In a real project you would run `go test` from the terminal and see PASS or FAIL for each test function. The structure is always the same.

title: t.Errorf — report a failure
instruction:
`t.Errorf` marks the test as failed and logs a message — but the test keeps running.

The message format is a convention: `"functionName(inputs) = actual, want expected"`

Add a second test case: call `add(-1, 1)` and verify it equals `0`. If it does not, use `t.Errorf` with the same format.
hint: got := add(-1, 1)  if got != 0 { t.Errorf("add(-1, 1) = %d, want 0", got) }

codeChecks.message: Call add(-1, 1) and capture the result.
Use t.Errorf to report if the result is wrong.
Replace the TODO comment with your test case.

successMessage: Two cases, one test function. Both pass. t.Errorf is how you say "this is wrong" without stopping the whole test — other cases still run and you see all failures at once.

title: t.Fatal — stop on first failure
instruction:
Sometimes a failure makes the rest of the test pointless. Use `t.Fatalf` to stop immediately.

For example: if you cannot connect to the database, there is no point running the next 20 queries.

Run this. Notice that when `divide` returns an error, `t.Fatalf` stops — the second check never runs. Then change `10, 0` to `10, 2` and see both checks pass.
hint: Change divide(10, 0) to divide(10, 2) in main's test call.
successMessage: t.Fatalf stops the test immediately — useful when a failure makes all subsequent assertions meaningless. t.Errorf continues — useful when you want to see all failures at once.

title: Table-driven tests
hint: Add {10, -3, 7} and {100, 200, 300} to the tests slice.

codeChecks.message: Add {10, -3, 7} to the test table.
Add {100, 200, 300} to the test table.
Replace the TODO comment with your two test cases.

successMessage: 5/5 passed. Table-driven tests are the gold standard in Go. Add a new case to the table, the loop handles it. No duplication, no copy-paste.

title: Testing error cases
instruction:
Your tests should cover failure paths too — not just the happy path.

For a function that returns `(result, error)`, test both:
1. When it should succeed — verify `err == nil` and check the result
2. When it should fail — verify `err != nil`

Add a test case for `divide(10, 0)` that checks an error is returned.
hint: { a: 10, b: 0, wantErr: true }  — in the loop: if tt.wantErr { if err == nil { t.Errorf(...) } } else { if err != nil { t.Errorf(...) } }

codeChecks.message: Add the error case: {10, 0, 0, true}
Replace the TODO comment with the error test case.

successMessage: 3/3 passed — including the error case. Always test failure paths. The bugs that hurt most are the ones in code nobody thought to test.

title: t.Run — subtests with names

codeChecks.message: Wrap each test case in t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }).
Use fmt.Sprintf to create the test name from tt.a and tt.b.
Replace the TODO comment with your t.Run call.

successMessage: Named subtests. When this fails in a real project, Go reports exactly which subtest failed: TestAdd/add(-5,5). No guessing which input broke.

title: Testing with interfaces — mocking
instruction:
Real code often depends on external things: databases, APIs, email services. You cannot call those in tests.

The solution: define the dependency as an interface. In tests, swap in a fake implementation.

Run this. `EmailSender` is an interface. `mockSender` is a fake that records calls instead of sending real email. The test verifies `SendWelcomeEmail` calls `Send` with the right message — no real email sent.
successMessage: No real email sent. The mock recorded the call, the test verified the behaviour. This pattern — interface + fake implementation — is how all professional Go testing works.

title: Benchmarks
successMessage: Both produce the same result but strings.Builder is significantly faster for large strings — it avoids creating a new string object on each iteration. Benchmarks reveal these differences.

title: Test helpers with t.Helper()
instruction:
When you have repeated assertion logic, extract it into a helper function and call `t.Helper()` inside it.

This tells Go: if this helper fails, report the line in the calling test — not the line inside the helper. Much easier to debug.

Run this. The `assertEqual` helper is used three times. Then add a fourth call that checks `multiply(3, 3)` equals `9`.

codeChecks.message: Add: assertEqual(t, multiply(3, 3), 9)
Replace the TODO comment with your assertion.

successMessage: t.Helper() is a small thing that makes debugging much easier. Without it, every failure points to line 12 (inside assertEqual). With it, Go points to the exact test line that failed.

title: Test a real function — isPalindrome
instruction:
Write tests for a function you define yourself.

`isPalindrome(s string) bool` returns `true` if the string reads the same forwards and backwards (case-insensitive).

`"racecar"` → true. `"hello"` → false. `"Racecar"` → true.

First implement `isPalindrome`. Then write a table-driven test with at least 5 cases.
hint: strings.ToLower + reverse the string and compare. Or: loop from both ends checking characters.

codeChecks.message: Implement the isPalindrome function.
Use strings.ToLower to make the check case-insensitive.
Add at least one palindrome and one non-palindrome test case.
Replace both TODO comments with your implementation and test cases.

successMessage: You implemented and tested a function from scratch. The test is the specification. If someone breaks isPalindrome later, your tests catch it immediately.

title: Fix the failing test
instruction:
The test exists. The function is broken.

`clamp(value, min, max int) int` should return:
- `min` if value is below min
- `max` if value is above max
- `value` otherwise

But the implementation is wrong. Fix `clamp` so all test cases pass.
hint: if value < min { return min }  if value > max { return max }  return value

codeChecks.message: Return min when value < min.
Return max when value > max.

successMessage: 5/5 passed. The test was right — the function was wrong. That is the value of tests: they are the objective referee. Fix the code, run the tests, trust the result.

title: Build a tested calculator
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a calculator with full test coverage:

1. Implement four functions: `add`, `subtract`, `multiply`, `divide` (returns `float64, error`)
2. Write a `TestCalculator` function with a table-driven test covering:
   - At least 2 cases per operation
   - The divide-by-zero error case
3. Use `t.Run` to name each subtest
4. Print the pass count at the end

Expected output ends with:
```
9/9 cases passed
```
(or more, depending on how many cases you add)
hint: Use a struct with fields: op string, a, b float64, want float64, wantErr bool. Switch on op to call the right function.

codeChecks.message: Implement the add function.
Implement the subtract function.
Implement the multiply function.
Implement the divide function.
Use t.Run to name each subtest.
Include a divide-by-zero error test case.
Print the pass count at the end.

successMessage: A fully tested calculator. Four functions, nine-plus test cases, named subtests, error coverage. This is production-quality Go code. You now test like a professional.
